questions.question: Explain the three main types of buses in a computer system and their functions.
keyPoints: DATA BUS: Carries actual data between CPU, memory, and I/O devices - bidirectional
ADDRESS BUS: Carries memory addresses from CPU to specify where data should go - unidirectional
CONTROL BUS: Carries control signals like READ, WRITE, INTERRUPT, CLOCK - manages bus operations

questions.question: What is bus arbitration? Describe any two methods of bus arbitration.
keyPoints: Bus arbitration resolves conflicts when multiple devices want to use the bus simultaneously
Centralized arbitration: Single arbiter grants bus access based on fixed/rotating priority
Distributed arbitration: Devices negotiate among themselves without central controller

questions.question: Calculate the bandwidth of a bus that operates at 200 MHz with a 32-bit width. Express your answer in MB/s.
keyPoints: Formula: Bandwidth = (Bus Width × Clock Rate) / 8
Calculation: (32 bits × 200 MHz) / 8 = 800 MB/s
Dividing by 8 converts bits to bytes

questions.question: Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous buses.
keyPoints: Synchronous: Uses common clock, fixed timing, simpler design, faster operation
Asynchronous: Uses handshaking signals, adapts to different device speeds, more flexible
Synchronous requires all devices to match clock speed; asynchronous is device-independent

questions.question: What are wait states? Why are they sometimes necessary in bus operations?
keyPoints: Wait states are additional clock cycles inserted when CPU must wait for slower devices
Needed when memory or I/O devices cannot keep up with CPU bus speed
Allows slower devices to complete operations, preventing data errors

questions.question: Explain the concept of bus bridging.
keyPoints: Bus bridging connects two different bus systems or standards
Allows devices from one bus type to communicate with devices on another
Example: PCI bridge connects CPU bus to PCI slots

questions.question: What is the difference between point-to-point and shared bus topology?
keyPoints: Point-to-point: Dedicated connection between two devices, no arbitration needed
Shared bus: Multiple devices use common pathway, requires arbitration
PCI Express uses point-to-point; traditional PCI uses shared bus

questions.question: With the help of a diagram, explain the structure of the system bus and describe how data transfer takes place between CPU and memory.
keyPoints: System bus consists of address, data, and control buses working together
CPU places address on address bus, sends control signals on control bus
Memory receives address, places data on data bus (read operation)
For write: CPU places address and data, sends WRITE control signal
Bus controller coordinates timing and ensures proper data transfer
Process involves address decoding, bus mastering, and data strobing

questions.question: Discuss the factors that affect bus performance and explain how each factor influences data transfer rate.
keyPoints: Bus width: More lines = more bits transferred per cycle (wider = faster)
Clock rate: Higher frequency = more transfers per second (faster = faster)
Bus arbitration method: Efficient arbitration reduces waiting time
Bus protocol efficiency: Overhead from control signals affects throughput
Number of devices: More devices = more contention and delays
Wait states: Inserted for slow devices, reduces effective bandwidth
Bus topology: Point-to-point vs shared affects scalability

questions.question: Explain the different bus standards used in modern computers. Compare PCI, PCI Express, and USB in terms of their characteristics and applications.
keyPoints: PCI (Peripheral Component Interconnect): Shared bus, parallel, 32/64-bit, used for expansion cards
PCI Express: Point-to-point serial, multiple lanes (x1, x4, x8, x16), replaced PCI/AGP
USB (Universal Serial Bus): External device connection, hot-swappable, multiple versions (2.0, 3.0, 4.0)
PCI: Internal expansion, high bandwidth for graphics, storage controllers
PCIe: Modern standard, scalable bandwidth, used for GPU, NVMe, networking
USB: Peripheral connectivity, plug-and-play, charging capability
